tree_title: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
overall_topic: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
questions: A multi-location gymnastics organization in the Northeastern United States offers competitive pathways that align with the USA Gymnastics level progression system, including: (1) a non-travel, in-house competitive option designed to prepare athletes for high school gymnastics; (2) a travel-based league that limits training to roughly 2–3 days per week to allow participation in other activities; and (3) a higher-commitment Junior Olympic track that may require up to 3–5 training days per week and leads to state, regional, and national championships. Identify this organization and name each of these three team program types. For each program, describe its competition structure and weekly training expectations. Additionally, list every gym location operated by this organization, including the city, state, and phone number for each facility.

Within the USA Gymnastics women’s development program, there are four major competitive tiers that progressively increase in difficulty and commitment. Identify each of these tiers and, for each one, specify: (1) the level numbers included, (2) whether routines are standardized or gymnast-designed, (3) the typical weekly training-hour range, (4) at least one representative key skill introduced in that tier, and (5) the highest type of championship competition athletes in that tier can ultimately qualify for. Provide a structured comparison covering all four tiers.

A parent-oriented guide to the USA Gymnastics level system opens with an empathetic introduction that acknowledges the strength, flexibility, and mental resilience required in the sport and invites families to continue reading for deeper insight. Separately, a gymnastics training center describes three distinct team pathways designed to accommodate different commitment levels, including an in-house option and two travel-based programs with varying weekly training demands. Based on publicly available information, summarize the purpose and tone of the introductory guide for parents, and then compare the three team pathways by outlining their structure, competition format, weekly training expectations, and overall goals for athlete development.

In the USA Gymnastics women’s level structure, the program is divided into four major tiers that progressively move athletes from foundational skill-building to the highest level of national and international competition. Identify each of these four tiers and, for every tier, explain (1) the level numbers included, (2) whether routines are non-competitive, standardized (compulsory), or athlete-designed (optional), (3) the typical weekly training hour range, and (4) how competition advancement works at that stage, including any progression toward state, regional, or national championships.

I cannot fully answer this query based on the search results provided. While the search results include information about New Hampshire Academy of Artistic Gymnastics (NHAAGS), established in 1973 as the first gymnastics organization in New Hampshire[1], the results do not contain sufficient details to identify the specific three program types you describe or provide a comprehensive list of all gym locations with phone numbers and contact information.

The available information confirms that NHAAGS offers "USAG Competitive gymnastics, Levels 3 through Elite and Xcel programs, little nhaags and Recreational classes, cheer tumbling and private lessons"[1], suggesting multiple program pathways. However, the search results lack:

- Specific names for the three distinct competitive pathways (non-travel, travel-based league, and Junior Olympic tracks)
- Detailed competition structures for each program tier
- Weekly training day expectations for each program level
- A complete directory of all gym locations operated by the organization with addresses and phone numbers

To properly answer this question, I would need access to the organization's current program guide or website with detailed facility information and program specifications. rounds.answer_b:
The organization is Atlantic Gymnastics Academy (AGA), a multi-location Northeastern U.S. provider aligned with USA Gymnastics level progression [1]. Its three team programs and details are: 1. In-House Competitive Team: Non-travel, high school prep-focused, with meets held exclusively at AGA facilities to build foundational competition skills; weekly training is 1–2 days [1]. 2. Travel League Team: Travel-based for regional meets, limiting training to 2–3 days/week to accommodate other activities, balancing competition exposure and personal commitments [1]. 3. Junior Olympic (JO) Team: Higher-commitment track with 3–5 weekly training days, leading to state, regional, and national USA Gymnastics championships, catering to advanced athletes [1]. AGA’s locations include: Portsmouth, NH (603-436-9711), Dover, NH (603-749-8181), and Exeter, NH (603-772-0088) [2]. Each facility offers program access with dedicated training spaces. The in-house team’s low-travel structure reduces logistical stress, while the travel league’s flexible schedule supports well-rounded development. The JO team’s intense training adheres to USA Gymnastics’ strict progression standards, ensuring skill mastery and eligibility for major championships, with coaches monitoring athlete progress to align with level-specific requirements for competitive advancement.

## References
[1] Atlantic Gymnastics Academy Official Website, https://www.atlanticgymnastics.com (accessed October 25, 2024)
[2] Atlantic Gymnastics Academy Locations Page, https://www.atlanticgymnastics.com/locations (accessed October 25, 2024)

Finally, User Experience: Agent B is more formatted and detailed, but the content is entirely about the wrong organization. Agent A is incomplete and unhelpful. Since both fail the fundamental identity requirement and provide unusable answers, this is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.question: Within the USA Gymnastics women’s development program, there are four major competitive tiers that progressively increase in difficulty and commitment. Identify each of these tiers and, for each one, specify: (1) the level numbers included, (2) whether routines are standardized or gymnast-designed, (3) the typical weekly training-hour range, (4) at least one representative key skill introduced in that tier, and (5) the highest type of championship competition athletes in that tier can ultimately qualify for. Provide a structured comparison covering all four tiers.
checklist_width: Developmental Levels (1–3): non-competitive foundation, 1–3 hours/week, example skill (e.g., forward roll, cartwheel, back handspring introduction), competition typically local (no championship pathway emphasized).
Compulsory Levels (4–5): standardized routines, 6–12 hours/week, example skill (e.g., back walkover, flyaway dismount, front handspring vault), pathway through state and regional championships.

Optional Levels (6–10): gymnast-designed routines within requirements, 12–30 hours/week (broken down as 12–20 for 6–8 and 20–30 for 9–10), example skill (e.g., giant swings, aerial cartwheel, multiple release moves), eligibility for National Championships at Levels 9–10.

Elite Level: beyond Level 10 (Junior Elite 11–15, Senior Elite 16+), highly individualized elite routines, 30–40 hours/week, advanced high-difficulty skills beyond Level 10, eligibility for national and international competitions including Olympics.
checklist_depth: Correct identification of the four tiers: Developmental (1–3), Compulsory (4–5), Optional (6–10), Elite (Junior/Senior) — matching level ranges accurately.
Clear logical linkage showing progression: foundational non-competitive → standardized compulsory competition → optional personalized routines → elite national/international pathway.
rounds.rationale: The question requires logical identification of the four official tiers of the USAG level structure without naming any source. It then demands aggregation of multiple attributes (levels, routine structure, hours, skills, and competition pathway) across all tiers. This ensures deep reasoning about classification and wide comparison of scattered program details.

rounds.answer_a:
The USA Gymnastics Women's Development Program comprises four progressive tiers[1][2]:

**Tier 1: Developmental (Levels 1–3)**
These levels use standardized coaching progressions but not standardized routines[1]. Representative skill: forward rolls and cartwheels (Level 2)[1]. Competition: In-house or state-level championships, if offered[4].

**Tier 2: Compulsory (Levels 4–5)**
All gymnasts perform identical routines nationally choreographed by USAG[1]. Representative skill: back hip circles on bars (Level 3–4 progression)[1]. Competition: State Championships[4].

**Tier 3: Optional (Levels 6–10)**
Gymnasts design routines within set difficulty minimums[1]. Levels 6–9 have difficulty restrictions; Level 10 has none[4]. Representative skill: release moves and advanced tumbling (Level 7)[1]. Competition: State Championships (Levels 6–7), Regional Championships (Level 8), and Eastern/Western Championships (Level 9) or National Championships (Level 10)[4].

**Tier 4: Elite (Above Level 10)**
Separate competitive track with distinct scoring and qualification pathways[2].

**Notable Gap:** The search results do not contain specific weekly training-hour ranges for each tier. Training hours typically increase with level progression but exact ranges require additional sources[1][4].

Advancement requires minimum mobility scores at sanctioned competitions, except Levels 1–3[4]. Gymnasts cannot skip levels except potentially Level 6[6].

## References

[1] https://stickitgirl.com/blogs/stickitgirl/womens-gymnastics-development-program-levels-guide

[2] https://usagym.org/women/elite/elite101/

[4] https://usagym.org/women/development/

[6] https://www.usagkentucky.org/development
rounds.answer_b:
Four USA Gymnastics women’s development program tiers (progressive difficulty/commitment) structured comparison:
1. Junior Developmental: (1) Levels 1-5; (2) Standardized routines [1]; (3) 3-6 weekly training hours [1]; (4) Forward roll on balance beam [1]; (5) State Developmental Championships [1].
2. Junior Elite: (1) Levels 6-10; (2) Gymnast-designed routines [2]; (3) 12-18 weekly training hours [2]; (4) Back handspring series on balance beam [2]; (5) Junior Olympic National Championships [2].
3. Senior Developmental: (1) Levels 11-12; (2) Gymnast-designed routines with coach guidance [3]; (3) 18-22 weekly training hours [3]; (4) Double back somersault on floor exercise [3]; (5) Senior Developmental National Championships [3].
4. Senior Elite: (1) Level 13; (2) Fully gymnast-designed routines [4]; (3) 25-30 weekly training hours [4]; (4) Yurchenko double full vault [4]; (5) U.S. National Championships (with Olympic Trials eligibility for top performers) [4]. Each tier advances foundational skills to elite mastery, with standardized routines giving way to personalized work and training hours rising to support complex skill acquisition and higher-stakes competition access.

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Junior Developmental Program Guidelines. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/developmental/junior-levels/
[2] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Junior Elite Program Handbook. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/elite/junior-elite/
[3] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Senior Developmental Program Overview. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/developmental/senior-levels/
[4] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Senior Elite Program Requirements. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/elite/senior-elite/
rounds.judge_reasoning:

Agent B invents incorrect tier groupings (e.g., Junior Developmental 1–5, Senior Developmental 11–12, Level 13), which do not reflect the actual USA Gymnastics framework. Thus, B fails the core entity check (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A omits required weekly training-hour ranges and incompletely specifies championship ceilings (especially for Elite) and key skills for every tier—this is a significant WIDE gap. Agent B, while structured, provides fabricated level ranges, hours, and championship pathways, compounding its structural error.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both use structured formatting, but Agent A’s structure aligns with the real program progression and would be more salvageable for a user. Despite missing required data, it preserves the correct developmental pathway. Because Agent A has notable omissions, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER; however, since Agent B fails the fundamental program structure, Agent A is clearly superior.

Therefore: A_BETTER.

rounds.answer_a:
The search results provided do not contain information about a parent-oriented guide with an empathetic introduction to the USA Gymnastics level system, nor do they describe three distinct team pathways at a specific training center with varying commitment levels and weekly training demands[1-8].

The available sources focus exclusively on the USA Gymnastics Women's Development Program's structural levels (1–10), skill progression, competition formats, and advancement requirements. They do not address:

- Parent-oriented guides or their tone and purpose
- In-house versus travel-based program structures
- Specific weekly training hour expectations across different pathways
- How training centers differentiate programs by commitment level

To answer your question comprehensively, I would need access to:

1. A specific parent guide published by USA Gymnastics or a gymnastics training center
2. Documentation of a training facility's team pathway offerings
3. Published training schedules or program descriptions outlining weekly hour commitments

The current search results establish that the Development Program exists as a unified national system with standardized progressions, but they do not contain information about how individual training centers structure their offerings around different family commitments or how they frame these options to parents.

To obtain this information, you could contact USA Gymnastics directly, consult specific training center websites or parent handbooks, or review facility-specific program materials that outline their team structure and weekly training expectations.

## References

[1] https://stickitgirl.com/blogs/stickitgirl/womens-gymnastics-development-program-levels-guide
[2] https://usagym.org/women/elite/elite101/
[3] https://chalkbucket.com/articles/gymnastics-levels-explained-womens-developmental-program.4/
[4] https://usagym.org/women/development/
[5] https://gmgc.com/understanding-usag-gymnastics-levels-a-complete-guide-2024-2/
[6] https://www.usagkentucky.org/development
[7] https://naigc.org/womens-rules/
[8] https://gymnasticshq.com/gymnastics-levels/
rounds.answer_b:
The parent-oriented USA Gymnastics level system guide’s core purpose is to demystify the sport’s structured progression framework, ease family uncertainty about tier-specific demands, and celebrate gymnasts’ often-overlooked strength, flexibility, and mental resilience while inviting deeper engagement with pathway details [1]. Its tone is warm, validating, and practical, framing guidance as collaborative support to help families make informed decisions about their child’s involvement rather than overwhelming them with jargon [1]. Three distinct training center team pathways (per a 2024 public center overview) are: 1. In-House Team: Structure (weekly group skill drills with basic coaching), competition format (low-pressure intra-center exhibitions), training (3-5 hours/week), goal (nurture recreational foundational skills for casual participation [2]. 2. Regional Travel Team: Structure (mixed group and private coaching sessions), competition format (regional qualify-only meets with limited travel), training (10-14 hours/week), goal (prepare athletes for Junior Developmental program tiers [2]. 3. National Travel Team: Structure (customized coaching blocks with specialized skill focus), competition format (national ranking meets with extensive travel), training (18-22 hours/week), goal (feed athletes into Senior Developmental or Elite program pathways [2].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Parent’s Guide to Women’s Artistic Gymnastics Level System. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/parent-resources/level-guide/
[2] Peak Performance Gymnastics Training Center. (2024). Team Pathways Program Overview. https://peakperformancegym.com/team-programs/pathways-and-commitments/

Thus, B also fails Deep Logic. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A provides none of the required checklist elements. Agent B provides a structured comparison, but the details (training hours, competition levels, pathway names) do not align with the expected publicly available descriptions, indicating major hallucinations. Both therefore fail WIDE aggregation.

User Experience: Agent B is better formatted and more comprehensive, but the content is likely inaccurate. Since accuracy is foundational and both responses fail core factual requirements (A through omission, B through hallucination), this results in a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024) As a parent or aspiring gymnast, navigating the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system can be both exciting and overw
rounds.answer_a:
**Tier 1: Developmental (Levels 1–3)**
Levels 1–3 focus on foundational skills with non-competitive or in-house competitive formats[1]. Gymnasts progress based on skill mastery rather than scored advancement; no minimum mobility score is required[4]. Progression is self-paced and achievement-oriented[4].

**Tier 2: Compulsory (Levels 4–5)**
Levels 4–5 introduce standardized, nationally choreographed routines where all gymnasts perform identical skills on all apparatus[1]. Level 4, the first required competitive level, requires a minimum mobility score of 34.0 all-around to advance to Level 5[2]. Judging emphasizes execution, precision, and consistency[1]. Gymnasts compete at State Championships[4].

**Tier 3: Optional (Levels 6–10)**
Levels 6–10 feature athlete-designed routines within difficulty restrictions (Levels 6–9 have set minimums; Level 10 has none)[4]. Advancement requires minimum scores at sanctioned competitions[4]. Competition pathways expand progressively: Levels 6–7 compete at State and Regional Championships; Level 8 at Regional Championships; Level 9 at Eastern/Western Championships; Level 10 culminates at WDP National Championships[4].

**Tier 4: Elite (Above Level 10)**
Athletes pursuing elite qualification follow separate competitive pathways distinct from the Development Program[1].

**Critical Data Gap:** Search results do not specify weekly training hour ranges for any tier. Training intensity typically increases with level progression, but exact ranges require supplemental sources.

## References

[1] https://stickitgirl.com/blogs/stickitgirl/womens-gymnastics-development-program-levels-guide

[2] https://chalkbucket.com/articles/gymnastics-levels-explained-womens-developmental-program.4/

[4] https://usagym.org/women/development/
rounds.answer_b:
Four USA Gymnastics women’s tiers (foundational to elite) structured comparison:
1. Junior Developmental: (1) Levels 1-5 [1]; (2) Standardized (compulsory) [1]; (3) 3-6 weekly training hours [1]; (4) Advance through local meet placements to state developmental championships, with no regional or national progression beyond this state-level cap [1].
2. Junior Olympic (Junior Elite): (1) Levels 6-10 [2]; (2) Athlete-designed (optional) [2]; (3) 12-18 weekly hours [2]; (4) Qualify for regional championships via strong state meet finishes, with top regional performers earning spots at the Junior Olympic National Championships [2].
3. Senior Developmental: (1) Levels 11-12 [3]; (2) Athlete-designed (optional) with coach guidance [3]; (3) 18-22 weekly hours [3]; (4) Progress from state to regional meets, then top regional finishers advance to the Senior Developmental National Championships [3].
4. Senior Elite: (1) Level 13 [4]; (2) Athlete-designed (optional, fully personalized) [4]; (3) 25-30 weekly hours [4]; (4) Qualify for U.S. National Championships via elite qualifiers, with top finishers moving to Olympic Trials or international competitions [4].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women’s Artistic Junior Developmental Program Guidelines. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/developmental/junior-levels/
[2] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women’s Artistic Junior Olympic Program Handbook. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/junior-olympic/
[3] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women’s Artistic Senior Developmental Program Overview. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/developmental/senior-levels/
[4] USA Gymnastics. (2024). Women’s Artistic Senior Elite Program Requirements. https://usagym.org/programs/womens/artistic/elite/senior-elite/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the four official tiers—Developmental (1–3), Compulsory (4–5), Optional (6–10), and Elite (beyond Level 10). Agent B invents an incorrect structure (e.g., Levels 1–5 as “Junior Developmental,” Levels 11–12 and 13 tiers), which does not match USA Gymnastics’ actual framework. Thus, B fails DEEP logic. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A omits major required elements—especially weekly training hour ranges for all tiers and specific Elite age breakdown (Junior/Senior Elite). That is a significant WIDE failure. However, Agent B not only misidentifies the tiers but also provides incorrect level groupings, fabricated advancement pathways, and inaccurate training ranges—failing BOTH deep structure and detailed aggregation.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A is clearly structured with bold tier headers and logical progression. Agent B is formatted clearly but built on an incorrect foundation, making it misleading despite good structure.

Conclusion: Although Agent A is incomplete, it correctly identifies the core structure, while Agent B fundamentally misrepresents the program. Therefore, Agent A wins, but only at the BETTER level due to substantial missing required details.
